perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” mean s all information and materials, in whatever form, whether tangible or intangible, disclosed by Companion Paws or The LifeLine Canada Foundation or any of its authorized representatives to Volunteer or Evaluator , or to which Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator otherwise gains access as a result of volunteering or w orking for Companion Paws or The LifeLine Canada Foundation , pertaining in any manner to the activities of C ompanion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation or its affiliates, consultants, members, or any person or entity to which Companion Paws ad The LifeLine Canada Foundation owes a duty of confidentiality, whether or not labeled or identified as proprietary or confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means only information and materials explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by Companion Paws or The LifeLine Canada Foundation. No other information, regardless of its nature or accessibility, shall be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition is broad, encompassing various types of information. The modified definition severely restricts what qualifies as confidential by requiring explicit written designation. This conflicts with established legal precedent regarding trade secrets and confidential information, which often protect information regardless of whether it's explicitly labeled.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification requires explicit labeling for information to be considered confidential. This contradicts the UTSA, which protects trade secrets based on their inherent confidentiality and competitive value, regardless of whether they are explicitly marked as 'Confidential.'

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by requiring explicit written designation for confidential information, contradicts the principle that confidentiality can exist regardless of labeling. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA protects trade secrets based on their inherent confidentiality, regardless of whether they are explicitly marked as 'Confidential'. The modified text attempts to narrow the scope of confidential information, creating a contradiction with the UTSA's broader protection.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator shall avoid personal and professional conflicts of interest in all matters pertaining to the Companion Paws Certification program. Conflicting interests may include but are not limite d to such areas as financial, personal relationships, and/or professional relationships.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator shall avoid only direct financial conflicts of interest, defined solely as receiving direct payments from competitors of Companion Paws. Other potential conflicts, including personal or professional relationships, are permissible.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowly defining 'conflicts of interest' to only direct financial benefits from competitors, this modification ignores other legally recognized conflicts, such as those arising from close personal relationships, which could unduly influence decisions and violate fiduciary duties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canada Not-for-profit Corporations Act, S.C. 2009, c. 23
perturbation.law_citation: Canada Not-for-profit Corporations Act, S.C. 2009, c. 23, s. 159(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits the definition of 'conflict of interest', whereas s. 159(1) of the Act requires directors to act honestly and in good faith with a view to the best interests of the corporation which includes avoiding conflicts of interest including those arising from close personal relationships.
perturbation.location: Section: Volunteer or Evaluator Conflict of Interest

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the general duty of directors to avoid conflicts of interest, as explained in the Law Explanation. By explicitly stating that only direct financial conflicts from competitors are prohibited, it implies that other conflicts (e.g., personal relationships) are permissible, which undermines the director's duty to act honestly and in good faith, thus creating a legal contradiction under Misaligned Terminology. The altered definition of 'conflict of interest' is narrower than generally accepted legal and fiduciary duties. Neither of the scraped laws are relevant to the task.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator acknowledges that a violation of the terms of this Agreement ma y cause damage and harm to Companion P aws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation and that any such damage or harm will be difficult if not impos sible to calculate in monetary terms and will be irre parable to Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation . Volunteer , Trainer and Evaluator agrees that, upon n otice from Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation declaring a breach of this A greement, Volunteer , Trainer or Evaluator shall immediately cease all further activities which are, or are claimed by Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation to be, a breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer, Trainer or Evaluator acknowledges that any alleged violation of this Agreement must result in proven quantifiable monetary damage exceeding $10,000 CAD to Companion Paws and The LifeLine Canada Foundation for any action to be taken. Furthermore, the volunteer is not required to stop their activities unless a court order is issued.
perturbation.explanation: The original text acknowledges that harm may be difficult to quantify. The modified text introduces a high threshold of 'proven quantifiable monetary damage' and removes the immediate cessation of activities upon notice. This conflicts with standard remedies for breach of contract, which often allow for injunctive relief even when monetary damages are difficult to precisely calculate, especially for breaches involving confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract (General Principle)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text sets a high bar for proving damages and requires a court order to cease activities, limiting the available remedies for breach. This contradicts general contract law principles which allow for a range of remedies including specific performance or injunctions in cases where monetary damages are inadequate or difficult to prove, particularly concerning confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a $10,000 CAD threshold for quantifiable monetary damage and requires a court order before activities must cease. This contradicts general contract law principles, specifically the availability of remedies such as specific performance or injunctions where monetary damages are inadequate or difficult to prove. The original text acknowledges the difficulty of calculating damages. The changed text makes it significantly more difficult to pursue a breach of contract claim. The LII/Legal Information Institute snippet states "Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance ." By setting a high monetary threshold and requiring a court order, the modified text severely limits the range of remedies available, creating a contradiction. The modified contract is therefore less enforceable.
